steps.title: Identify what the blank must do
steps.content: The sentence reads: "wildlife ______ is on the front lines of conservation efforts." The blank fills in the subject between "wildlife" and the verb "is." The answer choices all insert the title "biologist" followed by the proper name "Dr. Purnima Devi Barman." The grammatical question is whether and how to punctuate between the title and the name.

steps.title: Apply the restrictive appositive rule to eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — biologist, Dr. Purnima Devi Barman,: Two commas set off the name as nonessential (parenthetical) information. But the name here identifies which biologist is meant — it is essential, so commas that mark it as parenthetical are wrong. Rule violated: unnecessary nonrestrictive comma pair. ✗
  • Choice B — biologist, Dr. Purnima Devi Barman: An opening comma without a closing comma creates an unbalanced nonrestrictive appositive and leaves the subject poorly formed. Rule violated: unpaired comma creating a boundary error. ✗
  • Choice C — biologist: Dr. Purnima Devi Barman: A colon after "biologist" is not a standard way to introduce an appositive name that is the subject of the sentence; colons introduce lists or explanations, not subject appositives. Rule violated: incorrect use of colon with a subject appositive. ✗
  • Choice D — biologist Dr. Purnima Devi Barman: No punctuation is correct when an appositive is restrictive (essential). "Dr. Purnima Devi Barman" identifies the specific biologist, so no comma or colon should separate the title from the name. ✓
That matches Choice D — biologist Dr. Purnima Devi Barman.
